title: Building a Personal Portfolio with React and Vite
excerpt: A walkthrough of how I built this site using React 19, Vite, Tailwind CSS, and MongoDB hosted on Vercel.

children.text: When it came time to revamp my personal site, I wanted something fast, modern, and easy to maintain. The stack I landed on was React 19 + Vite + Tailwind CSS on the frontend, and MongoDB Atlas with Vercel serverless functions on the backend.
Why Vite?
Vite's dev server startup is near-instant, and its build output is lean. Coming from Create React App, the improvement in DX is significant.
Tailwind for styling
Tailwind lets me design directly in JSX without context-switching to CSS files. The utility-first approach keeps styles co-located with components.
MongoDB for content
Storing experience, skills, and now blog posts in MongoDB means I can update content without a redeployment. A quick seed script syncs local JSON files to the database.
The end result is a site that loads fast, looks clean, and is simple to extend — which is exactly what you're reading right now!

title: Cycling in Helsinki: Routes I Recommend
excerpt: Helsinki is a fantastic cycling city. Here are some of my favourite routes that I ride regularly.

children.text: I've been cycling in Helsinki for several years now and the city keeps getting better for bikes. Here are a few routes I keep coming back to.
Baana
The Baana is a dedicated cycling highway that runs through the city centre. It's fast, flat, and well-maintained all year round — even in winter when the city ploughs it first.
Keskuspuisto loop
Heading north through Töölönlahti and into Keskuspuisto gives you a slice of forest right inside the city. The trails are mostly gravel and wonderfully quiet.
Espoo coastal route
On longer weekend rides I head west along the coast towards Espoo. The views over the archipelago are worth every kilometre.
If you're visiting Helsinki and enjoy cycling, I highly recommend renting a Fillarit city bike and just exploring — the city is very bikeable.

title: Making Music as a Side Project
excerpt: How I balance software development with music production, and what I've learnt from treating creativity as play rather than work.

children.text: I've been producing music under the name Ronsu for years, and it remains one of my favourite ways to unwind after a day of coding.
The overlap between code and music
Both disciplines reward patience and iteration. A great melody, like a good architecture, rarely appears fully formed — it's refined over many sessions.
My setup
I keep things simple: a DAW, a MIDI controller, and a decent pair of headphones. Gear doesn't make the music; curiosity does.
Sharing on SoundCloud
SoundCloud remains my favourite place to post tracks. The community is genuine, and the feedback loop — posting something rough and getting honest reactions — is invaluable.
You can listen to my tracks on the Music page of this site. I hope something resonates with you!
